sub_criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
sub_criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision focused on Explainable AI (XAI) in mechanical ventilation weaning, with a defined career trajectory towards a Clinician Scientist role and Chair. The training plan is well-justified with specific courses in qualitative methods and implementation science. Strong mentorship is evidenced by a multidisciplinary supervisory team with extensive track records (>£5M grants, >80 publications) and a structured weekly meeting framework.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive' nature of the ideas (g.1.b) relies heavily on the novelty of the XAI approach which is well-supported but could be more explicitly contrasted with existing non-explainable CDSS in the vision statement itself, though the detailed plan covers this.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive' nature of the ideas (g.1.b) relies heavily on the novelty of the XAI approach which is well-supported but could be more explicitly contrasted with existing non-explainable CDSS in the vision statement itself, though the detailed plan covers this.

sub_criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
sub_criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
sub_criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant clearly articulates why this award is the right next step, positioning it as a springboard to gain expert skills in mixed methods and ML to transition from a trainee to an independent researcher. The award is justified as necessary to secure protected time for developing new skills in ML and implementation, building directly on existing data science and clinical experience. It explicitly details how the award will develop research partnerships within the NHS (Guy's & St Thomas', Royal Brompton) and industry.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the justification is comprehensive and directly addresses all sub-signals with specific examples of skill acquisition and partnership development.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the justification is comprehensive and directly addresses all sub-signals with specific examples of skill acquisition and partnership development.

sub_criteria.name: Relevant Research Experience and Training
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates prior research experience and/or training that prepares the applicant to successfully undertake the proposed award in applied health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of previous projects, research methods, or techniques relevant to the proposed study
Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant lists formal qualifications (MSc, FRCA, MRCP) and extensive clinical/research roles (Academic Clinical Fellow, Fellowships). The research background details specific prior projects (GEMINI, narrative reviews) and formal training (MSc Health Data Science, MSc Medical Education) directly relevant to the proposed study. The training plan includes specific planned courses in qualitative methods and ML, demonstrating a strong alignment between prior experience and the skills required for the proposed project.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the evidence of relevant experience and training is robust and well-documented across multiple sections.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the evidence of relevant experience and training is robust and well-documented across multiple sections.

sub_criteria.name: Impactful Research Outputs and Outcomes
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence of meaningful research outputs or outcomes appropriate to the applicant’s career stage, demonstrating contribution to the field.

signals.signal_text: Number and quality of publications, reports, or presentations
Citation metrics or other indicators of impact (where mentioned)
Awards, grants, or recognitions linked to prior research
Description of practical or societal impact of previous work

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant provides a substantial list of peer-reviewed publications in high-impact journals (e.g., Anaesthesia, BMJ, Lancet) and multiple awards (prizes, bursaries, HEA Fellowship). The research background lists 12 publications, 12 abstracts, and a co-edited book, demonstrating a strong track record. The practical impact is highlighted through policy engagement and the development of clinical guidelines.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While citation metrics are not explicitly quantified in the text (g.4.b), the high-impact journal list serves as a strong proxy. The description of societal impact is present but could be more quantified in terms of patient numbers reached by previous work.
sub_criteria.rationale: While citation metrics are not explicitly quantified in the text (g.4.b), the high-impact journal list serves as a strong proxy. The description of societal impact is present but could be more quantified in terms of patient numbers reached by previous work.

sub_criteria.name: Trajectory Towards Research Leadership
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates potential and progression towards becoming a future leader in health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Statements of career ambitions and long-term goals
Evidence of increasing responsibility in previous roles (e.g., leading projects, mentoring)
Participation in leadership or professional development programs
Recognition by peers or institutions suggesting leadership potential

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant shows clear career ambitions to become a Clinician Scientist and Chair. Evidence of increasing responsibility is demonstrated through roles as Trainee Editor, Chair of Pan-London Network, and EDI Representative, alongside peer review activities. Recognition by peers is evident through multiple prizes and leadership in the GEMINI project.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Participation in formal leadership programs (g.5.c) is less explicitly detailed compared to the practical leadership roles held, though the training plan addresses this gap.
sub_criteria.rationale: Participation in formal leadership programs (g.5.c) is less explicitly detailed compared to the practical leadership roles held, though the training plan addresses this gap.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Practitioner-Academic Career (if applicable)
sub_criteria.definition: For applicants including a clinical or practice element, shows a clear commitment to combining research with professional practice.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of practitioner-academic career intentions
Involvement in clinical or practice-based research
Evidence of balancing practice responsibilities with research activities
Participation in relevant professional networks or qualifications

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly states a commitment to a dual specialist clinical academic track, balancing registrar training with research. The CV demonstrates a clear practitioner-academic trajectory with concurrent clinical training (Anaesthetics/ICM) and dedicated research posts. The research background explicitly states the goal of becoming a Clinician Scientist, balancing full-time clinical work with research training and policy engagement.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the commitment is clear, the specific mechanisms for balancing the heavy clinical workload of an ST7 registrar with the demands of a 3-year fellowship are implied but could be more explicitly detailed in terms of protected time allocation strategies.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the commitment is clear, the specific mechanisms for balancing the heavy clinical workload of an ST7 registrar with the demands of a 3-year fellowship are implied but could be more explicitly detailed in terms of protected time allocation strategies.

sub_criteria.name: Quality of the Plain English Summary
sub_criteria.definition: Clear, simple description of the research that a non-specialist audience can understand; explains problem, importance, plan, beneficiaries, and real-world use; avoids jargon; aligns with the detailed proposal.

signals.signal_text: Readability scores (e.g., Flesch-Kincaid)
Low density of unexplained jargon or technical terms
Presence of problem statement, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, and real-world impact
Alignment with detailed proposal content
Sentence structure and coherence for non-specialist understanding
Emphasis on societal or patient relevance

sub_criteria.pros: The Plain English Summary demonstrates exceptional readability with a Flesch-Kincaid grade of 5.51 and low jargon density (1.77%). It clearly articulates the problem, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, and impact, with specific evidence of iterative refinement based on patient and public feedback to remove jargon.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While alignment with the detailed proposal is strong, the lexical overlap metric (0.121) suggests the summary is highly condensed, which is appropriate but leaves little room for nuance in the lay description compared to the technical depth of the full plan.
sub_criteria.rationale: While alignment with the detailed proposal is strong, the lexical overlap metric (0.121) suggests the summary is highly condensed, which is appropriate but leaves little room for nuance in the lay description compared to the technical depth of the full plan.

sub_criteria.name: Evidence Review Quality & Relevance
sub_criteria.definition: How comprehensively and critically the application reviews existing literature and evidence.

signals.signal_text: Number and relevance of references cited
Presence of critical analysis vs. mere summary
Identification of knowledge gaps
Coherence and logical flow

sub_criteria.pros: The application includes a comprehensive narrative review of 2,616 studies, critically analyzing heterogeneity in weaning practices. It clearly identifies the 'black box' limitation of existing CDSS as a specific knowledge gap, justifying the need for explainable AI (XAI).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The review is described as a narrative review rather than a systematic review, which may limit the comprehensiveness of the evidence base compared to a formal systematic approach, though it is sufficient for a fellowship proposal.
sub_criteria.rationale: The review is described as a narrative review rather than a systematic review, which may limit the comprehensiveness of the evidence base compared to a formal systematic approach, though it is sufficient for a fellowship proposal.

sub_criteria.name: Research Scope & Award Appropriateness
sub_criteria.definition: Whether the project scope matches the scale and expectations of the proposed award (doctoral, postdoctoral, etc.).

signals.signal_text: Alignment of aims with award level
Feasibility indicators (time, complexity, tasks)
Proportion of work relative to award expectations

sub_criteria.pros: The project scope is highly appropriate for a 36-month fellowship, featuring three distinct, manageable objectives: model development, co-design of visual tools, and simulation testing. The timeline is realistic, with specific data sources (MIMIC-IV, NHS trusts) and risk mitigation strategies included.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks identified; the scope is well-calibrated to the award duration and complexity.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks identified; the scope is well-calibrated to the award duration and complexity.

sub_criteria.name: Research Design & Methodological Rigor
sub_criteria.definition: Clarity, appropriateness, and robustness of study design to answer the research questions.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of methods
Logical alignment between aims and methods
Discussion of controls, sample size, or methodology justification
Structured methodological sections

sub_criteria.pros: The research design is rigorous, specifying three neural network architectures (TFT, TCN, LSTM) with 5-fold cross-validation. The human-AI interaction study uses a modified between-subjects design with a power calculation (n=102) and physiological measures of cognitive load, ensuring robust methodology.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The reliance on simulated vignettes (WP3) rather than live clinical trials may limit the generalizability of findings to real-world chaotic ICU environments, though this is a standard and appropriate approach for a feasibility study.
sub_criteria.rationale: The reliance on simulated vignettes (WP3) rather than live clinical trials may limit the generalizability of findings to real-world chaotic ICU environments, though this is a standard and appropriate approach for a feasibility study.

sub_criteria.name: Potential Impact on Patients, Public, and Health Services
sub_criteria.definition: Likely benefits or influence of the research on patients, carers, or health and care systems.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of direct or indirect patient/public benefits
Alignment with health priorities or societal needs
Clarity and specificity of expected outcomes

sub_criteria.pros: The application explicitly links research outcomes to tangible patient benefits (reduced ventilator time, improved comfort) and NHS benefits (reduced length of stay, cost optimization). It addresses high-priority health needs regarding inconsistent weaning practices.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The impact is projected based on the successful development of the tool; the actual clinical implementation and long-term cost savings are not yet realized, which is expected for a research proposal.
sub_criteria.rationale: The impact is projected based on the successful development of the tool; the actual clinical implementation and long-term cost savings are not yet realized, which is expected for a research proposal.

sub_criteria.name: Engagement with People & Communities
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence of meaningful involvement of patients, carers, or communities.

signals.signal_text: Use of co-design, participatory methods, or consultations
References to ethical considerations and feedback mechanisms
Description of involvement at multiple project stages

sub_criteria.pros: Patient and public involvement is deeply integrated, featuring a 12-member Patient and Family Advisory Group (PFAG), a public co-applicant, and co-design workshops. Specific feedback from these groups has already shaped recruitment criteria, data security measures, and the visual design of the tool.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the engagement is extensive and spans the entire research lifecycle.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the engagement is extensive and spans the entire research lifecycle.

sub_criteria.name: Justification of Resources & Award Costs
sub_criteria.definition: Appropriateness and clarity of requested resources for the planned work.

signals.signal_text: Explicit links between tasks and resources requested
Budget proportionality to project scope
Clear rationale for funding level

sub_criteria.pros: The budget is highly detailed with explicit justifications linking costs to specific tasks (e.g., GPU for data analysis, travel for collaboration, PPIE meetings). The proportionality of costs to the project scope is clear, with specific line items for clinician time, database access, and training.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the budget is well-justified and aligned with the work packages.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the budget is well-justified and aligned with the work packages.

sub_criteria.name: Quality of Previous Research (if applicable)
sub_criteria.definition: Prior relevant research experience demonstrating capability to deliver the proposed project.

signals.signal_text: Number and relevance of publications or outputs
Evidence of prior successful projects
Skills alignment with proposed work

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant demonstrates a strong track record with prior successful projects (GEMINI, ventilation reviews) and relevant skills in machine learning and qualitative methods. The alignment between prior experience and the proposed work is explicit and robust.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the applicant's background strongly supports the feasibility of the proposed research.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the applicant's background strongly supports the feasibility of the proposed research.

sub_criteria.name: Inclusive Research Design
sub_criteria.definition: Consideration of inclusivity throughout the research lifecycle, including participant diversity and accessibility.

signals.signal_text: Explicit strategies for diverse recruitment
Adaptive methods or accessibility considerations
Equity and participation indicators

sub_criteria.pros: The application commits to assessing bias in under-represented groups, adhering to SAGER guidelines and the NIHR-INCLUDE framework. It includes purposive sampling for diversity in qualitative components and a diverse PFAG (25% non-white) to ensure inclusive design.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the plan for inclusivity is strong, the actual diversity of the final dataset (MIMIC-IV and NHS data) depends on the source data's representativeness, which is outside the applicant's direct control but is mitigated by the bias assessment plan.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the plan for inclusivity is strong, the actual diversity of the final dataset (MIMIC-IV and NHS data) depends on the source data's representativeness, which is outside the applicant's direct control but is mitigated by the bias assessment plan.

sub_criteria.name: Quality and Relevance of Proposed Training
sub_criteria.definition: How well-structured, clear, and relevant the training plan is in relation to the project’s goals and the applicant’s research needs.

signals.signal_text: Completeness and clarity of training description
Alignment of training topics with project methods or techniques
Structured timeline or sequencing of training activities
Explicit links between training content and skill acquisition

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides a highly detailed and costed training plan listing specific courses (e.g., 'Qualitative Research Methods in Health' at UCL, 'An Introduction to Implementation Science' at KCL, 'Experience-Based Co-design' by Point of Care Foundation) and platforms (Coursera, Datacamp). The plan explicitly links these activities to skill acquisition in qualitative methodology, implementation science, and machine learning, directly addressing the project's mixed-methods requirements.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the plan lists specific courses and their relevance, it lacks a granular timeline or sequencing (e.g., which courses occur in which year of the PhD), preventing a perfect score for structured timeline (td.1.c).
sub_criteria.rationale: While the plan lists specific courses and their relevance, it lacks a granular timeline or sequencing (e.g., which courses occur in which year of the PhD), preventing a perfect score for structured timeline (td.1.c).

sub_criteria.name: Suitability to Personal Development and Project Needs
sub_criteria.definition: How well the proposed training addresses the applicant’s current skills gaps and supports successful project delivery.

signals.signal_text: Identification of personal skill gaps
Clear mapping between training activities and project tasks
Evidence that proposed training is targeted rather than generic
Mention of mentorship, workshops, or formal courses relevant to development needs

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly identifies skill gaps in qualitative methodology, implementation science, and co-design, noting that previous reviewer feedback highlighted these as areas needing detail. The training plan is highly targeted, mapping specific external courses to these gaps (e.g., UCL course for qualitative methods, Glasgow course for complex interventions) rather than offering generic training. Mentorship is integrated through the supervisory team's expertise in these specific areas.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details found; the identification of gaps and the mapping to specific, costed training activities are comprehensive and directly evidenced.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details found; the identification of gaps and the mapping to specific, costed training activities are comprehensive and directly evidenced.

sub_criteria.name: Support for Career Aspirations
sub_criteria.definition: The extent to which the training plan, and any professional practice components, contribute to the applicant’s long-term career goals.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of career goals
Connections between training activities and future career trajectory
Indications of skill transferability beyond the current project
Reference to professional practice or experiential learning where applicable

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant clearly states long-term career goals: to become a Clinician Scientist, lead a research group, and attain a Chair in anaesthesia and critical care. The training plan is explicitly framed as a 'springboard' to achieve these goals, with specific connections made between the fellowship's protected time, the acquisition of mixed-methods skills, and the transition to independence. The plan also highlights the development of transferable skills (e.g., data visualization, implementation science) that extend beyond the immediate project to support future leadership roles.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details found; the connection between the training activities and the long-term career trajectory is explicit and well-supported by the text.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details found; the connection between the training activities and the long-term career trajectory is explicit and well-supported by the text.

sub_criteria.name: Track Record of the Contracting Organisation (and Partners)
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrated expertise, experience, and research achievements of the organisation(s) in the proposed research area.

signals.signal_text: Publication or project history in relevant research fields
Previous funding success or awards in similar domains
Mention of prior collaborations or partnerships
Highlighted achievements or notable outputs

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides extensive evidence of the contracting organisation's (Imperial College London) and partners' track record. Supervisors have significant funding success (e.g., >£5M, >£14M), high publication counts (>80, >150), and international recognition. Specific prior collaborations (Aalborg University, NHS trusts) and notable outputs (published reviews, clinical trials) are detailed, demonstrating deep expertise in the proposed research area.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the evidence for track record is comprehensive and specific.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the evidence for track record is comprehensive and specific.

sub_criteria.name: Suitability of Supervisory Support and Mentorship Arrangements
sub_criteria.definition: Quality, clarity, and appropriateness of supervision and mentoring for supporting the applicant’s research and career development.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of supervisor roles and responsibilities
Evidence of mentorship plans, meetings, or training support
Alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs
Presence of structured supervision framework (frequency, format, accountability)

sub_criteria.pros: The supervisory team is explicitly described with clear roles: Dr. Patel (clinical oversight, weekly meetings), Dr. Ramnarayan (paediatric/AI), Dr. Ni (methodology/implementation), and Dr. Serban (ML/visualisation). The expertise aligns perfectly with the project's multidisciplinary needs. A structured framework is evident, including weekly meetings and specific mentorship plans within the Imperial DSI and NIHR HRC.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the supervision plan is robust and well-defined.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the supervision plan is robust and well-defined.

sub_criteria.name: Appropriateness of the Contracting Organisation to Support the Award and Career Aspirations
sub_criteria.definition: How well the organisation (and any partners) can provide the infrastructure, resources, and environment necessary to deliver the project and support the applicant’s career growth.

signals.signal_text: Description of facilities, infrastructure, or resources provided
Links between organisational support and project success
Alignment of organisational focus with applicant’s career trajectory
Evidence of tailored support for award type (doctoral, postdoctoral)

sub_criteria.pros: The application details access to critical infrastructure: secure NHS data environments (BDAU SE), Google Cloud for MIMIC-IV, and the Imperial Data Science Institute (DSI) Data Observatory. Links between organisational support (NIHR HRC, DSI) and project success are explicit. The organisation's focus on AI and critical care aligns with the applicant's career trajectory, and tailored support for a doctoral fellowship (CA TO, mentorship) is confirmed.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; infrastructure and support are clearly articulated and sufficient.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; infrastructure and support are clearly articulated and sufficient.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Inclusive and Supportive Research Culture
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence that the organisation fosters inclusivity, research integrity, and a supportive environment for all researchers.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of policies or practices promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion
Evidence of inclusive recruitment, mentoring, or support initiatives
References to adherence to research integrity principles
Statements on organisational culture, training, or initiatives supporting underrepresented groups

sub_criteria.pros: The Host Organisation Support Statement explicitly lists multiple EDI accreditations (Athena Swan Gold, Race Equality Charter, Time to Change, Disability Confident, AccessAble, Stonewall), demonstrating a strong commitment to an inclusive culture. The statement describes a diverse community where everyone is treated with respect. The applicant also mentions adherence to research integrity principles (SAGER, NIHR-INCLUDE) in the research plan.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the organisational accreditations are strong (ss.4.a, ss.4.d), the specific mention of 'research integrity principles' is more tied to the applicant's plan than a distinct organisational policy statement in the support letter, though the letter implies a supportive environment. This minor distinction prevents a perfect 5 for ss.4.c, though the evidence is still very strong.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the organisational accreditations are strong (ss.4.a, ss.4.d), the specific mention of 'research integrity principles' is more tied to the applicant's plan than a distinct organisational policy statement in the support letter, though the letter implies a supportive environment. This minor distinction prevents a perfect 5 for ss.4.c, though the evidence is still very strong.

sub_criteria.name: Integration of Lived Experience in Research Design
sub_criteria.definition: How input from patients, carers, or people with relevant lived experience has shaped the research questions, design, and methods.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of consultation or co-design activities
Specific examples of changes made to study design based on lived experience
References to patient or community advisory groups

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates exceptional integration of lived experience. A 12-member Patient and Family Advisory Group (PFAG) and a public co-applicant were actively involved from the proposal stage, leading to specific design changes such as refining recruitment criteria, addressing data security concerns, and co-designing visualizations and the user interface. The PFAG also guides dissemination strategies.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the evidence for integration is comprehensive and spans the entire research lifecycle.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the evidence for integration is comprehensive and spans the entire research lifecycle.

sub_criteria.name: Diversity and Inclusivity of the Research Team
sub_criteria.definition: Extent to which the research team reflects diversity in terms of skills, experience, and demographic representation.

signals.signal_text: Explicit mentions of team composition across gender, ethnicity, age, or other relevant characteristics
Statements about inclusion in hiring or team-building
Evidence of equitable task allocation and collaboration

sub_criteria.pros: No specific evidence found in the provided text regarding the demographic diversity (gender, ethnicity, age) of the research team itself, nor explicit statements on inclusive hiring practices or equitable task allocation within the team.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The application lacks explicit details on the diversity of the research team composition. While the PFAG is noted to be diverse (25% non-white), there is no corresponding information provided about the diversity of the academic and clinical researchers leading the project.
sub_criteria.rationale: The application lacks explicit details on the diversity of the research team composition. While the PFAG is noted to be diverse (25% non-white), there is no corresponding information provided about the diversity of the academic and clinical researchers leading the project.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
sub_criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

sub_criteria.pros: A robust plan exists to maximize public and patient benefit. The PFAG is actively involved in crafting messages for public dissemination and will co-present results at conferences. The plan includes high-impact publications, code sharing on Zenodo, and engagement with media teams and charities to ensure actionable outcomes reach the intended audiences.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the dissemination plan is detailed, costed, and directly links study objectives to anticipated benefits for patients and the public.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the dissemination plan is detailed, costed, and directly links study objectives to anticipated benefits for patients and the public.

sub_criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
sub_criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

sub_criteria.pros: The application explicitly addresses representation of seldom-heard groups. Feedback from the RPG highlighted concerns about homogeneity, leading to a targeted strategy to ensure the PFAG includes 25% non-white members and is UK-wide. Recruitment criteria were also adjusted to ensure inclusivity.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the PFAG diversity is well-documented, the specific strategies for ensuring diversity among the 102 clinicians participating in the vignette study (WP3) are less detailed, though general commitment to fairness is stated.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the PFAG diversity is well-documented, the specific strategies for ensuring diversity among the 102 clinicians participating in the vignette study (WP3) are less detailed, though general commitment to fairness is stated.

sub_criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

sub_criteria.pros: Inclusivity is demonstrated across the entire research lifecycle. Engagement began during proposal development (RPG/PERC), continues through a structured 4-monthly advisory group for ongoing input, and extends to dissemination where patients co-present results. Specific feedback loops are documented.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the evidence clearly shows inclusion is embedded in planning, conduct, and dissemination phases.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the evidence clearly shows inclusion is embedded in planning, conduct, and dissemination phases.

sub_criteria.name: Costed and Resourced Plan for Research Inclusion
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence that research inclusion has been properly budgeted and resourced within the proposal.

signals.signal_text: Explicit budget line(s) for inclusion activities (PPI, outreach, recruitment support)
Mention of staff time, materials, or training dedicated to inclusion
Alignment between inclusion plan and allocated resources

sub_criteria.pros: The plan for research inclusion is fully costed and resourced. The budget explicitly includes £11,580 for PPIE activities, covering meetings, preparation time, travel, and internet access for the PFAG and co-applicant, in accordance with NIHR guidelines. This ensures the inclusion activities are sustainable and not reliant on voluntary labor.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the budget alignment with the inclusion plan is explicit and comprehensive.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the budget alignment with the inclusion plan is explicit and comprehensive.

sub_criteria.name: Use of Formatting, Headings, and Subheadings
sub_criteria.definition: Effective use of text formatting to make the application clear, navigable, and easy to read.

signals.signal_text: Presence and consistency of headings and subheadings
Use of bold or emphasis for key terms
Structured hierarchy of sections (logical nesting of headings)
Patterns detected via text parsing for formatting markers

sub_criteria.pros: The application form analysis confirms a robust structural hierarchy with 10 detected sections, 35 numbered heading-like lines, and 44 list markers, indicating consistent use of headings and subheadings. The presence of 26 extracted emphasis markers and 147 table-like budget lines demonstrates effective use of formatting to enhance navigability and clarity.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While emphasis markers are detected, the analysis notes a parser limitation where bold/emphasis may be lost during text extraction; therefore, the score for emphasis usage is capped at 4 to account for potential under-detection rather than absence.
sub_criteria.rationale: While emphasis markers are detected, the analysis notes a parser limitation where bold/emphasis may be lost during text extraction; therefore, the score for emphasis usage is capped at 4 to account for potential under-detection rather than absence.

sub_criteria.name: Limited Duplication and Repetition
sub_criteria.definition: Avoidance of unnecessary repetition within or across sections.

signals.signal_text: Semantic similarity measures between sentences/paragraphs
Detection of repeated phrases or ideas
Novelty score for each section (unique content vs. overlap)

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates low duplication rates, with a duplicate sentence rate of 0.014 and a repeated line rate of 0.069. Lexical overlap between major sections (e.g., Research Background and Training Support at 0.272) is moderate and likely reflects necessary thematic consistency rather than unnecessary repetition.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While duplication is low, the lexical overlap between 'Applicant Research Background' and 'Training & Development' (0.272) and 'Detailed Research Plan' (0.231) suggests some repetition of content across sections, preventing a perfect score of 5.
sub_criteria.rationale: While duplication is low, the lexical overlap between 'Applicant Research Background' and 'Training & Development' (0.272) and 'Detailed Research Plan' (0.231) suggests some repetition of content across sections, preventing a perfect score of 5.

sub_criteria.name: Logical Flow and Coherence Across Sections
sub_criteria.definition: The application progresses logically, with a clear thread linking the research question, objectives, and work packages.

signals.signal_text: Semantic coherence between sections (using embeddings or similarity metrics)
Explicit linking of objectives to methods and work packages
Presence of transitional phrases or linking statements
Detectable narrative thread anchored on research aims

sub_criteria.pros: The application exhibits strong logical flow with 32 transition phrases and 127 objective-method-budget link terms. The section order follows a coherent narrative from applicant context to research plan, PPI, training, and budget, ensuring a clear thread linking research questions to work packages.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats or missing details regarding logical flow were identified in the structural analysis; the evidence is complete and specific.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats or missing details regarding logical flow were identified in the structural analysis; the evidence is complete and specific.
